design_philosophy.core_loop: Seasonal resource allocation and military strategy: distribute soldiers between fiefs, manage economy, maintain peasant loyalty, engage in diplomacy, and conduct turn-based warfare to conquer Japan during the Sengoku period
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing military expansion with economic sustainability and peasant loyalty—aggressive policies (higher taxes, cultivation) boost productivity but erode loyalty and risk rebellion; defensive policies preserve stability but slow conquest
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Rise as a feudal daimyo (or assume the role of Oda Nobunaga) to unify fragmented Japan through strategic conquest, diplomacy, and resource management
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The game treats conquest as a multi-faceted challenge requiring economic and social management, not just military power. Peasant loyalty and resource constraints force players to think beyond battlefield tactics. Multiple victory conditions and selectable starting daimyo encourage varied approaches to unification

difficulty.checkpointing: Campaign progression saved between turns; player can reload to undo poor decisions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects from four campaign scenarios with different starting dates (1560–1582) and difficulty tiers; scenario selection determines enemy strength and time pressure

progression.upgrade_system: Military training increases fighting efficiency; flood control and town expansion unlock higher tax revenues; peasant/soldier recruitment scales with available resources
progression.unlock_mechanism: Conquered fiefs unlock new territories for resource extraction and military deployment; diplomatic pacts and marriages unlock alliances; technology progression through merchant system (weapons, supplies)
replayability_hooks: Four distinct campaign scenarios with different start dates and strategic positions
Choice of multiple daimyo (not locked to Oda Nobunaga)
Multiple victory conditions (enemy retreat, command unit destruction, supply attrition, prolonged standoff)
Dynamic peasant loyalty system creates emergent challenges based on player decisions

world_building_techniques: Historical Sengoku period setting with real daimyo and provinces
Scenario structure references historical campaigns (1560 'Battle for the East', 1582 'Road Towards Unification')
Fief names and territorial divisions reflect actual feudal Japan geography

faction_indicators: Rival daimyo with distinct territories and military strength
Alliance and marriage pacts reflect historical power networks
danger_signals: Peasant unrest due to high taxes or cultivation
Low peasant loyalty can trigger rebellion in fiefs
Enemy spying (player can spy on rivals; inverse is true)
environmental_storytelling: Fief prosperity reflected in tax revenue and peasant population
Military unit placement shows expansion progress across map
Seasonal turn structure mirrors historical campaign seasons

sources_used: wikipedia: Nobunaga's Ambition series overview and gameplay mechanics
